paragraphs.en: Previous waves of automation — from the mechanical loom to the assembly line to the spreadsheet — followed a recognisable pattern: they displaced workers from specific tasks while creating new categories of work that absorbed the displaced labour. The weaver replaced by the power loom found work in the mill; the bookkeeper replaced by the spreadsheet became an analyst; the typist replaced by the word processor became an administrator with a broader remit. Each transition was painful, and each was eventually absorbed.
paragraphs.fr: Les vagues précédentes d'automatisation — du métier à tisser mécanique à la chaîne de montage en passant par le tableur — ont suivi un schéma reconnaissable : elles ont déplacé les travailleurs de tâches spécifiques tout en créant de nouvelles catégories de travail qui ont absorbé la main-d'œuvre déplacée. Le tisserand remplacé par le métier à tisser mécanique a trouvé du travail dans l'usine ; le comptable remplacé par le tableur est devenu analyste ; la dactylo remplacée par le traitement de texte est devenue administratrice avec un rôle plus large. Chaque transition a été douloureuse, et chacune a finalement été absorbée.

paragraphs.en: The question being asked with increasing urgency is whether the current wave of automation — driven by large language models, computer vision, and robotics — follows the same pattern, or whether it is qualitatively different. Previous automation displaced routine physical labour and, to some extent, routine cognitive labour. The new technologies are moving into domains that were long considered immune: creative work, analytical reasoning, communication, care.
paragraphs.fr: La question posée avec une urgence croissante est de savoir si la vague actuelle d'automatisation — tirée par les grands modèles de langage, la vision par ordinateur et la robotique — suit le même schéma, ou si elle est qualitativement différente. L'automatisation précédente a déplacé le travail physique de routine et, dans une certaine mesure, le travail cognitif de routine. Les nouvelles technologies se déplacent vers des domaines longtemps considérés comme immunisés : le travail créatif, le raisonnement analytique, la communication, le soin.

paragraphs.en: The optimistic view holds that this is more of the same: new technologies will create categories of work we cannot currently imagine, as they always have. The pessimistic view holds that this time is different: that the breadth and speed of the displacement is unprecedented, that the new work being created requires skills that most displaced workers cannot readily acquire, and that the transition period — even if it eventually resolves — will be long enough to cause enormous social dislocation.
paragraphs.fr: La vision optimiste soutient que c'est encore la même chose : les nouvelles technologies créeront des catégories de travail que nous ne pouvons pas actuellement imaginer, comme elles l'ont toujours fait. La vision pessimiste soutient que cette fois c'est différent : que l'ampleur et la vitesse du déplacement sont sans précédent, que le nouveau travail créé nécessite des compétences que la plupart des travailleurs déplacés ne peuvent pas facilement acquérir, et que la période de transition — même si elle se résout éventuellement — sera suffisamment longue pour causer d'énormes perturbations sociales.

paragraphs.en: There is a third question, less often asked, which may be the most important. Even if automation creates sufficient new work to absorb displaced labour — even if the optimists are correct about the quantity — the nature of that work matters. A society in which the remaining human work consists primarily of monitoring algorithms, personalising experiences, and servicing the needs generated by automated production is a society that has answered the question of employment without answering the question of meaning.
paragraphs.fr: Il y a une troisième question, moins souvent posée, qui peut être la plus importante. Même si l'automatisation crée suffisamment de nouveau travail pour absorber la main-d'œuvre déplacée — même si les optimistes ont raison sur la quantité — la nature de ce travail compte. Une société dans laquelle le travail humain restant consiste principalement à surveiller des algorithmes, personnaliser des expériences et répondre aux besoins générés par la production automatisée est une société qui a répondu à la question de l'emploi sans répondre à la question du sens.

paragraphs.en: Work is not merely economic. It is one of the primary ways human beings structure time, build identity, contribute to communities, and find purpose. The communities most devastated by deindustrialisation — in the Rust Belt, in former mining towns, in textile regions — did not suffer primarily from income loss, though that was severe. They suffered from the collapse of the social structures and individual purposes that work had organised. The opioid crisis in former industrial communities is not separable from the loss of meaningful work.
paragraphs.fr: Le travail n'est pas simplement économique. C'est l'une des façons principales dont les êtres humains structurent le temps, construisent l'identité, contribuent aux communautés et trouvent un but. Les communautés les plus dévastées par la désindustrialisation — dans la Rust Belt, dans les anciens villages miniers, dans les régions textiles — n't ont pas principalement souffert de pertes de revenus, bien que celles-ci aient été sévères. Elles ont souffert de l'effondrement des structures sociales et des buts individuels que le travail avait organisés. La crise des opioïdes dans les anciennes communautés industrielles n'est pas séparable de la perte d'un travail porteur de sens.

paragraphs.en: None of this suggests that automation is bad, or that we should resist it. It suggests that the conversation about automation needs to be considerably broader than it currently is — that 'what happens to jobs?' is not the same question as 'what happens to people?', and that answering the first without addressing the second is insufficient. A universal basic income would address income loss; it would not by itself address the loss of structure, purpose, and social integration that work currently provides. Those are harder problems, and they are the ones that matter most.
paragraphs.fr: Rien de tout cela ne suggère que l'automatisation est mauvaise, ou que nous devrions y résister. Cela suggère que la conversation sur l'automatisation doit être considérablement plus large qu'elle ne l'est actuellement — que « qu'arrive-t-il aux emplois ? » n'est pas la même question que « qu'arrive-t-il aux gens ? », et que répondre à la première sans traiter la seconde est insuffisant. Un revenu universel de base répondrait à la perte de revenus ; il ne résoudrait pas en soi la perte de structure, de but et d'intégration sociale que le travail fournit actuellement. Ce sont des problèmes plus difficiles, et ce sont ceux qui comptent le plus.

vocabulary.definition_en: le retrait de quelque chose de sa place habituelle ; (économie du travail) la perte d'emplois due à l'automatisation ou à d'autres changements structurels
vocabulary.example: Labour displacement from automation has historically been absorbed by new industries.

vocabulary.definition_en: la réduction de l'activité industrielle, en particulier manufacturière, dans une région ou une économie
vocabulary.example: Deindustrialisation has devastated former manufacturing communities across the developed world.

vocabulary.definition_en: perturbation des structures sociales, des relations et des normes qui donnent leur cohérence aux communautés
vocabulary.example: Economic shocks can cause social dislocation that outlasts the economic crisis itself.

vocabulary.definition_en: le domaine de responsabilité ou le périmètre d'activité attribué à une personne ou une organisation
vocabulary.example: Her role evolved to include a broader remit than originally described.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui ne s'est jamais produit ou n'a jamais existé auparavant
vocabulary.example: The speed and breadth of AI-driven automation is arguably unprecedented.

vocabulary.definition_en: protégé d'une maladie ou d'une influence ; non susceptible
vocabulary.definition_fr: immunisé, protégé de, à l'abri de
vocabulary.example: Creative work was once considered immune to automation.

vocabulary.definition_en: une politique de versements réguliers et inconditionnels en espèces à tous les citoyens, quel que soit leur emploi
vocabulary.example: Universal basic income is one proposed response to labour displacement from automation.

vocabulary.definition_en: l'épidémie de dépendance et de décès par surdose liés aux analgésiques opioïdes et à l'héroïne, particulièrement sévère dans les anciennes communautés industrielles américaines
vocabulary.example: Research links the opioid crisis directly to the loss of manufacturing employment.

vocabulary.definition_en: l'étendue ou la portée de quelque chose ; largeur
vocabulary.example: The breadth of domains affected by AI is what makes this wave different.

vocabulary.definition_en: la capacité à décomposer des problèmes complexes et à évaluer les preuves de manière systématique
vocabulary.example: Large language models are beginning to compete in domains requiring analytical reasoning.

grammar_points.title: 'Even if X — even if the optimists are correct about the quantity — the quality matters'
grammar_points.explanation: La **double concession** (« even if X... even if Y... ») reconnaît pleinement une objection potentielle avant de formuler une affirmation plus forte qui la transcende. Elle signale que votre argument ne dépend pas de la victoire dans le débat précédent.
examples: '**Even if** automation creates sufficient new work — **even if** the optimists are correct about quantity — the **nature** of that work matters.'
'**Even if** the policy reduces poverty — **even if** the numbers improve — the loss of dignity it entails would still matter.'
'**Granting that** X is true, and **granting further** that Y is true, the conclusion does not follow.'
This structure shows you've anticipated and acknowledged the strongest counterargument.

grammar_points.title: 'What happens to jobs?' is not the same question as 'What happens to people?'
grammar_points.explanation: Utiliser des **questions intégrées comme sujets** est une manière formelle et précise de séparer deux questions souvent confondues. La structure parallèle force une comparaison.
examples: '**What happens to jobs?** is not the same question as **what happens to people?**'
'**Whether the policy is popular** is a different question from **whether it is correct**.'
'**How much it costs** and **whether it is worth the cost** are separate questions.'
This structure is excellent for analytical writing that refuses to let complexity collapse into simplicity.

grammar_points.title: 'It would address X; it would not by itself address Y' — the 'not by itself' qualification
grammar_points.explanation: **« Not by itself »** signale que quelque chose est nécessaire mais insuffisant — que cela résout partiellement un problème mais laisse un résidu. C'est plus précis que de dire « ça ne résoudrait pas tout ».
examples: 'A universal basic income would address income loss; it would **not by itself** address loss of purpose.'
'Regulation would reduce harm; it would **not by itself** build trust.'
'Investment in education is necessary; it is **not by itself** sufficient.'
'Not by itself' is a more elegant formulation than 'alone' or 'on its own'.

expressions.example: The pessimists argue that this time is different — the breadth of displacement is unprecedented.
expressions.note: Une expression utilisée (et surutilisée) pour décrire chaque vague de changement technologique. Sa crédibilité dépend de la spécification exacte du comment et du pourquoi.

expressions.example: Previous automation displaced workers while creating new work that absorbed the displaced labour.
expressions.note: Langage économique pour désigner la création de nouveaux emplois remplaçant ceux détruits par l'automatisation. L'hypothèse optimiste sous-jacente à la plupart de l'histoire économique de la technologie.

expressions.expression: answering the question of employment without answering the question of meaning
expressions.meaning_fr: répondre à la question de l'emploi sans répondre à la question du sens
expressions.example: A society can answer the employment question without answering the meaning question.
expressions.note: La distinction clé de l'essai — revenu/emploi et sens/but sont des choses différentes souvent confondues dans le débat politique.

expressions.example: The opioid crisis is not separable from the loss of meaningful work.
expressions.note: Utilisé pour insister sur un lien causal ou constitutif entre deux choses. Plus fort que « connected to » — implique qu'on ne peut pas les comprendre séparément.

expressions.example: Communities in the Rust Belt suffered social dislocation as much as economic loss.
expressions.note: L'ancien cœur industriel du nord-est des États-Unis (Ohio, Pennsylvanie, Michigan), autrefois défini par l'acier et l'automobile, aujourd'hui associé au déclin économique.

written.question: What is the 'third question' the author identifies as most important?
options: Whether automation will eventually be reversible.
Whether new work will be created in sufficient quantity.
Whether the new work will provide meaning and purpose, not just income.
Whether governments will be able to retrain displaced workers.
written.explanation: 'Even if automation creates sufficient new work... the nature of that work matters.' A society that answers the employment question without answering the meaning question is insufficient.

written.question: What does the author say would be insufficient about a universal basic income as a response to automation?
options: It would be too expensive to fund.
It would reduce the incentive to work.
It would address income loss but not the loss of structure, purpose, and social integration.
It would only benefit people in wealthy countries.
written.explanation: 'A universal basic income would address income loss; it would not by itself address the loss of structure, purpose, and social integration that work currently provides.'

written.sentence: ________ ________ the economic figures improve, the loss of community purpose remains a separate problem.
written.hint: A double concession structure that acknowledges a point before making a stronger one.

written.sentence: Better data would reduce uncertainty; it would ________ ________ ________ eliminate the need for judgement.
written.hint: A phrase meaning 'alone' or 'without other elements — partially but not fully'.

written.sentence: The mental health crisis in depressed communities is not ________ ________ the decline of meaningful employment.
written.hint: A phrase meaning 'inseparably linked to, cannot be understood in isolation from'.

written.french: Les nouvelles technologies pénètrent des domaines longtemps considérés comme immunisés contre l'automatisation : le raisonnement analytique, le travail créatif, le soin.
written.suggested_answer: The new technologies are moving into domains long considered immune to automation: analytical reasoning, creative work, care.

written.french: La question 'qu'arrive-t-il aux emplois ?' n'est pas la même que 'qu'arrive-t-il aux personnes ?', et répondre à la première sans traiter la seconde est insuffisant.
written.suggested_answer: The question 'what happens to jobs?' is not the same as 'what happens to people?', and answering the first without addressing the second is insufficient.
key_vocabulary: answering the first without addressing the second

written.french: Les communautés dévastées par la désindustrialisation ont souffert de l'effondrement des structures sociales que le travail avait organisées, pas seulement de la perte de revenus.
written.suggested_answer: Communities devastated by deindustrialisation suffered from the collapse of the social structures that work had organised, not just from income loss.

oral.instruction: Read the paragraph on work and meaning. The tone is calm, clear and quietly insistent — this is an argument being made with conviction, not alarm.
oral.text: Work is not merely economic. It is one of the primary ways human beings structure time, build identity, contribute to communities, and find purpose. The communities most devastated by deindustrialisation did not suffer primarily from income loss, though that was severe. They suffered from the collapse of the social structures and individual purposes that work had organised.
oral.focus: The contrast 'did not suffer primarily from income loss, though that was severe' is the pivot. Give 'though that was severe' its own quiet weight — it acknowledges something real before redirecting.

oral.question: Is the current wave of automation genuinely different from previous ones, or are we repeating the same historical anxiety? What evidence would settle the debate?
oral.suggested_answer: My view is that the current wave is probably different in degree, though whether it's different in kind remains genuinely unclear. What distinguishes it is the breadth of domains affected — previous automation was largely confined to physical routine tasks, then extended to cognitive routine tasks. Large language models are beginning to compete in domains that required flexible language reasoning, creative synthesis and contextual judgement, which were previously considered definitively human. Whether this leads to permanent structural unemployment or simply a faster and more painful transition than previous ones depends partly on policy responses and partly on factors we can't yet predict. The evidence that would settle it: a persistent rise in long-term unemployment in highly educated occupations despite economic growth, with no corresponding new category of work emerging over a sustained period. We haven't seen that yet, but we're perhaps earlier in the transition than the debate acknowledges.
vocabulary_hints: different in degree vs different in kind

oral.question: If automation does eliminate many jobs, how should society respond? What policies would you prioritise, and what do you think they would miss?
oral.suggested_answer: I'd prioritise three things. First, a genuine — not token — investment in retraining that is delivered at working-age scale rather than in pilot programmes. Second, a serious examination of reduced working hours rather than full employment maintained through increasingly marginal jobs: distributing work differently might be more sustainable than creating work for its own sake. Third, and perhaps most fundamentally, a much broader conversation about what activities we value and whether we can find ways to recognise and resource them — care, community work, creativity — that currently fall outside the market. What would these policies miss? The deeper point from the essay: work isn't primarily about income. Even if you solve the income problem and the time problem, you haven't necessarily solved the meaning problem. That requires cultural change as much as policy change, and cultural change is harder to engineer.
vocabulary_hints: activities we value outside the market
